(2) Survival period (typically 12-24 months general; longer for fundamental reps and tax/environmental). (3) Caps and baskets (typical: 10-15% cap on general reps with $X deductible/tipping basket). (4) Carve-outs from cap (fraud, fundamental reps, intentional misrepresentation). (5) Exclusive remedy provision (subject to fraud carve-out). (6) Indemnification escrow / RWI insurance allocation.
mock_tool_return.common_variations: Sandbagging vs anti-sandbagging; DE default permits sandbagging absent contractual language otherwise.
mock_tool_return.drafting_notes: DE permits broad freedom of contract. Eureka Holdings v. Verizon and ABRY Partners line addresses fraud carve-out scope. Be explicit about pre-closing tax indemnity treatment. Consider RWI as cap supplement vs replacement.

tasks.user_message: MAC/MAE clause — what's the standard structure for a buyer-favorable target MAC out in M&A

mock_tool_return.standard_text: Standard MAC: (1) any change/event/effect that has had or would reasonably be expected to have a material adverse effect on the business, results of operations, or financial condition of the target (taken as a whole). (2) Standard exclusions: general economic conditions; industry-wide effects; war/terrorism; pandemic; changes in law/GAAP; failure to meet projections (alone). tasks.user_message: Article III standing — current requirements after TransUnion

mock_tool_return.holding_summary: Article III standing requires: (1) CONCRETE injury — either tangible (physical, monetary) or intangible with a 'close relationship' to a harm traditionally recognized at common law (e.g., reputational harm, breach of confidence); (2) traceability to defendant's conduct; (3) redressability by judicial decision. Bare statutory violations alone insufficient. Risk of future harm requires that materialization be imminent, not speculative. In class actions, each member must individually satisfy standing for damages claims.

tasks.user_message: 'good faith' in commercial context — UCC definition, anything specific to know

mock_tool_return.definition: UCC § 1-201(20) defines 'good faith' as 'honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing'. Subjective (honesty in fact) AND objective (reasonable commercial standards) elements both required. Pre-2001 UCC limited good faith to honesty-in-fact only for non-merchants; current UCC (2001 amendment) extends commercial-standards element to all parties.
mock_tool_return.jurisdiction_specific_note: All 49 UCC states (Louisiana excepted) adopt 2001 amendment. NY codifies separately at NY UCC § 1-201.

tasks.user_message: 'foreseeable damages' in NY contract law — give me the precise definition for the brief

mock_tool_return.definition: Hadley v. Baxendale (1854) framework adopted by NY: damages recoverable in contract are limited to those (a) arising naturally from breach (general), or (b) reasonably contemplated by both parties at contract formation as probable consequence of breach (consequential). NY codifies via NY UCC § 2-715. NY courts strictly enforce notice-or-knowledge requirement for consequential damages.
mock_tool_return.source: Hadley v. Baxendale, 9 Ex. 341 (1854); NY UCC § 2-715; Kenford Co. v. County of Erie, 73 N.Y.2d 312 (1989)
mock_tool_return.jurisdiction_specific_note: NY courts particularly strict on consequential-damages notice; Kenford established that lost-profits require 'reasonable certainty' standard plus parties' contract-formation contemplation.

tasks.user_message: 'transferred intent' in tort law — definition and current scope

mock_tool_return.definition: When a defendant commits a tortious act intending to harm one party but harms a different party, intent transfers to the actually-harmed party. Restatement (Second) of Torts § 16 governs — applies to battery, assault, false imprisonment, trespass to chattels, trespass to land. NOT applicable to negligence. Limited to the same intentional-tort categories.
mock_tool_return.source: Restatement (Second) of Torts § 16; Restatement (Third) of Torts: Liability for Physical and Emotional Harm (continued recognition)
mock_tool_return.jurisdiction_specific_note: Universal across US jurisdictions for the five enumerated intentional torts; jurisdiction-variation primarily on transfer-between-different-tort-categories (e.g., shooter intends battery, victim hit with assault — minority view permits transfer).

tasks.user_message: Williston on Contracts — discussion of unconscionability post-Williams v. Walker-Thomas, give me the section

arguments.query: Williston Contracts unconscionability Williams Walker Thomas procedural substantive

mock_tool_return.summary: Williston catalogs the procedural-substantive unconscionability framework synthesized from Williams v. Walker-Thomas Furniture (D.C. Cir. 1965) and UCC § 2-302. Procedural prong: oppression / surprise in formation (e.g., adhesion contract, unequal bargaining); substantive prong: terms substantively oppressive or one-sided. Most state courts require BOTH (though some apply sliding-scale: more of one means less of other required).
mock_tool_return.current_status: current updated treatise; competing with Corbin
